perf_counter_en[\"\\System\\Context Swit.description:
Context Switches/sec is the combined rate at which all processors on the computer are switched from one thread to another.
Context switches occur when a running thread voluntarily relinquishes the processor, is preempted by a higher priority ready thread, or switches between user-mode and privilege

perf_counter_en[\"\\Processor Informatio.name: CPU user time
perf_counter_en[\"\\Processor Informatio.description: The processor information `% User Time` counter shows the percent of time that the processor(s) is spent executing in User mode.

perf_counter_en[\"\\Processor Informatio.name: CPU privileged time
perf_counter_en[\"\\Processor Informatio.description: The processor information `% Privileged Time` counter shows the percent of time that the processor is spent executing in Kernel (or Privileged) mode. Privileged mode includes services interrupts inside Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs), executing Deferred Procedure Calls (DPCs), Device Driver calls

perf_counter_en[\"\\Processor Informatio.name: CPU interrupt time
perf_counter_en[\"\\Processor Informatio.description: The processor information `% Interrupt Time` counter indicates how much time the processor spends handling hardware interrupts during sample intervals. It reflects the activity of devices like the system clock, mouse, disk drivers, and network cards. A value above 20% suggests possible hardware issu

perf_counter_en[\"\\Processor Informatio.name: CPU DPC time
perf_counter_en[\"\\Processor Informatio.description: Processor DPC time is the time that a single processor spent receiving and servicing deferred procedure calls (DPCs). DPCs are interrupts that run at a lower priority than standard interrupts. `% DPC Time` is a component of `% Privileged Time` because DPCs are executed in privileged mode. If a high

perf_counter_en[\"\\Paging file(_Total)\.name: Used swap space in %
perf_counter_en[\"\\Paging file(_Total)\.description: The used space of swap volume/file in percent.

perf_counter_en[\"\\Memory\\Pool Nonpage.name: Memory pool non-paged
perf_counter_en[\"\\Memory\\Pool Nonpage.description:
This measures the size, in bytes, of the non-paged pool. This is an area of system memory for objects that cannot be written to disk but instead must remain in physical memory as long as they are allocated.
There is a possible memory leak if the value is greater than 175MB (or 100MB with the /3GB s

perf_counter_en[\"\\Memory\\Pages/sec\"].name: Memory pages per second
perf_counter_en[\"\\Memory\\Pages/sec\"].description:
This measures the rate at which pages are read from or written to disk to resolve hard page faults.
If the value is greater than 1,000, as a result of excessive paging, there may be a memory leak.

perf_counter_en[\"\\Memory\\Page Faults/.name: Memory page faults per second
perf_counter_en[\"\\Memory\\Page Faults/.description: Page Faults/sec is the average number of pages faulted per second. It is measured in number of pages faulted per second because only one page is faulted in each fault operation, hence this is also equal to the number of page fault operations. This counter includes both hard faults (those that requir

perf_counter_en[\"\\Memory\\Free System.name: Free system page table entries
perf_counter_en[\"\\Memory\\Free System.description: This indicates the number of page table entries not currently in use by the system. If the number is less than 5,000, there may be a memory leak or you running out of memory.

perf_counter_en[\"\\Memory\\Cache Bytes\.name: Cache bytes
perf_counter_en[\"\\Memory\\Cache Bytes\.description: Cache Bytes is the sum of the Memory\\System Cache Resident Bytes, Memory\\System Driver Resident Bytes, Memory\\System Code Resident Bytes, and Memory\\Pool Paged Resident Bytes counters. This counter displays the last observed value only; it is not an average.

oracle.database_wait_time_ratio.description: Wait time - the time that the server process spends waiting for available shared resources to be released by other server processes such as latches, locks, data buffers, etc.

oracle.db_files_limit.description: The maximum allowable number of datafiles.

oracle.disk_sorts.description: The number of sorts going to disk per second.

oracle.fra_number_of_files.description: The number of files in the FRA.

oracle.pga_target.description: The current value of the `PGA_AGGREGATE_TARGET` initialization parameter. If this parameter is not set, then its value is "0" and automatic management of the PGA memory is disabled.

oracle.fra_restore_point.name: FRA, Number of restore points
oracle.fra_restore_point.description: Number of restore points in the FRA.

oracle.fra_space_limit.description: The maximum amount of disk space (in bytes) that the database can use for the Fast Recovery Area (FRA).

oracle.fra_space_reclaimable.description: The total amount of disk space (in bytes) that can be created by deleting obsolete, redundant, and other low-priority files from the FRA.

oracle.fra_space_used.description: The amount of disk space (in bytes) used by FRA files created in the current and all the previous FRAs.

oracle.fra_usable_pct.description: Percentage of space usable in the FRA.

oracle.gc_cr_block_received_rate.description: The global cache (GC) and the consistent read (CR) block received per second.

oracle.instance_hostname.description: The name of the host machine.

oracle.instance.role.description: Indicates whether the instance is an active instance or an inactive secondary instance.

oracle.library_cache_hit_ratio.name: Library cache hit ratio
oracle.library_cache_hit_ratio.description: The ratio of library cache hits (Hits/Pins).

oracle.long_table_scans_rate.description: The number of long table scans per second. A table is considered long if it is not cached and if its high water mark is greater than five blocks.

oracle.pga_global_bound.description: The maximum size of a work area executed in automatic mode.

mssql.autoparam_attempts_sec.rate.name: Auto-param attempts per second
mssql.autoparam_attempts_sec.rate.description: Number of auto-parameterization attempts per second. The total should be the sum of the failed, safe, and unsafe auto-parameterizations. Auto-parameterization occurs when an instance of SQL Server tries to parameterize a Transact-SQL request by replacing some literals with parameters so that reuse o

mssql.average_latch_wait_time_raw.name: Average latch wait time raw
mssql.average_latch_wait_time_raw.description: Average latch wait time (in milliseconds) for latch requests that had to wait.

mssql.average_wait_time_raw.name: Total average wait time raw
mssql.average_wait_time_raw.description: Average amount of wait time (in milliseconds) for each lock request that resulted in a wait. Information for all locks.

mssql.batch_requests_sec.rate.name: Batch requests per second
mssql.batch_requests_sec.rate.description: Number of Transact-SQL command batches received per second. This statistic is affected by all constraints (such as I/O, number of users, cache size, complexity of requests, and so on). High batch requests mean good throughput.

mssql.buffer_cache_hit_ratio.name: Buffer cache hit ratio
mssql.buffer_cache_hit_ratio.description: Indicates the percentage of pages found in the buffer cache without having to read from the disk. The ratio is the total number of cache hits divided by the total number of cache lookups over the last few thousand page accesses. After a long period of time, the ratio changes very little. Since readi

mssql.cache_hit_ratio.name: Cache hit ratio
mssql.cache_hit_ratio.description: Ratio between cache hits and lookups.

mssql.cache_objects_in_use.name: Cache objects in use
mssql.cache_objects_in_use.description: Number of cache objects in use.

mssql.cache_object_counts.name: Cache object counts
mssql.cache_object_counts.description: Number of cache objects in the cache.

mssql.cache_pages.name: Cache pages
mssql.cache_pages.description: Number of 8-kilobyte (KB) pages used by cache objects.

mssql.checkpoint_pages_sec.rate.name: Checkpoint pages per second
mssql.checkpoint_pages_sec.rate.description: Indicates the number of pages flushed to the disk per second by a checkpoint or other operation which required all dirty pages to be flushed.

mssql.database_pages.name: Database pages
mssql.database_pages.description: Indicates the number of pages in the buffer pool with database content.

mssql.data_files_size.name: Total data file size
mssql.data_files_size.description: Total size of all data files.

mssql.failed_autoparams_sec.rate.name: Failed auto-params per second
mssql.failed_autoparams_sec.rate.description: Number of failed auto-parameterization attempts per second. This number should be small. Note that auto-parameterizations are also known as simple parameterizations in the newer versions of SQL Server.

mssql.forwarded_records_sec.rate.name: Forwarded records per second
mssql.forwarded_records_sec.rate.description: Number of records per second fetched through forwarded record pointers.

mssql.free_list_stalls_sec.rate.name: Free list stalls per second
mssql.free_list_stalls_sec.rate.description: Indicates the number of requests per second that had to wait for a free page.

mssql.full_scans_sec.rate.name: Full scans per second
mssql.full_scans_sec.rate.description: Number of unrestricted full scans per second. These can be either base-table or full-index scans. Values greater than 1 or 2 indicate that there are table / index page scans. If that is combined with high CPU, this counter requires further investigation, otherwise, if the full scans are on small tab

mssql.granted_workspace_memory.name: Granted Workspace Memory
mssql.granted_workspace_memory.description: Specifies the total amount of memory currently granted to executing processes, such as hash, sort, bulk copy, and index creation operations.

mssql.index_searches_sec.rate.name: Index searches per second
mssql.index_searches_sec.rate.description: Number of index searches per second. These are used to start a range scan, reposition a range scan, revalidate a scan point, fetch a single index record, and search down the index to locate where to insert a new row.

mssql.latch_waits_sec.rate.name: Latch waits per second
mssql.latch_waits_sec.rate.description: The number of latch requests that could not be granted immediately. Latches are lightweight means of holding a very transient server resource, such as an address in memory.

mssql.lazy_writes_sec.rate.name: Lazy writes per second
mssql.lazy_writes_sec.rate.description: Indicates the number of buffers written per second by the buffer manager's lazy writer. The lazy writer is a system process that flushes out batches of dirty, aged buffers (buffers that contain changes that must be written back to the disk before the buffer can be reused for a different page) and ma

mssql.lock_requests_sec.rate.name: Total lock requests per second
mssql.lock_requests_sec.rate.description: Number of new locks and lock conversions per second requested from the lock manager.

mssql.lock_timeouts_sec.rate.name: Total lock requests per second that timed out
mssql.lock_timeouts_sec.rate.description: Number of timed out lock requests per second, including requests for NOWAIT locks.

mssql.lock_waits_sec.rate.name: Total lock requests per second that required waiting
mssql.lock_waits_sec.rate.description: Number of lock requests per second that required the caller to wait.

mssql.lock_wait_time.name: Lock wait time
mssql.lock_wait_time.description: Average of total wait time (in milliseconds) for locks in the last second.

mssql.logins_sec.rate.name: Logins per second
mssql.logins_sec.rate.description: Total number of logins started per second. This does not include pooled connections. Any value over 2 may indicate insufficient connection pooling.

mssql.logouts_sec.rate.name: Logouts per second
mssql.logouts_sec.rate.description: Total number of logout operations started per second. Any value over 2 may indicate insufficient connection pooling.

mssql.log_files_size.name: Total log file size
mssql.log_files_size.description: Total size of all the transaction log files.

mssql.log_files_used_size.name: Total log file used size
mssql.log_files_used_size.description: The cumulative size of all the log files in the database.

mssql.maximum_workspace_memory.name: Maximum workspace memory
mssql.maximum_workspace_memory.description: Indicates the maximum amount of memory available for executing processes, such as hash, sort, bulk copy, and index creation operations.

mssql.memory_grants_outstanding.name: Memory grants outstanding
mssql.memory_grants_outstanding.description: Specifies the total number of processes that have successfully acquired a workspace memory grant.

mssql.memory_grants_pending.name: Memory grants pending
mssql.memory_grants_pending.description: Specifies the total number of processes waiting for a workspace memory grant.

mssql.number_deadlocks_sec.rate.name: Total lock requests per second that have deadlocks
mssql.number_deadlocks_sec.rate.description: Number of lock requests per second that resulted in a deadlock.

mssql.page_life_expectancy.name: Page life expectancy
mssql.page_life_expectancy.description: Indicates the number of seconds a page will stay in the buffer pool without references.

mssql.page_lookups_sec.rate.name: Page lookups per second
mssql.page_lookups_sec.rate.description: Indicates the number of requests per second to find a page in the buffer pool.

mssql.page_reads_sec.rate.name: Page reads per second
mssql.page_reads_sec.rate.description: Indicates the number of physical database page reads that are issued per second. This statistic displays the total number of physical page reads across all databases. As physical I/O is expensive, you may be able to minimize the cost either by using a larger data cache, intelligent indexes, and more

mssql.page_splits_sec.rate.name: Page splits per second
mssql.page_splits_sec.rate.description: Number of page splits per second that occur as a result of overflowing index pages.

mssql.page_writes_sec.rate.name: Page writes per second
mssql.page_writes_sec.rate.description: Indicates the number of physical database page writes that are issued per second.

mssql.readahead_pages_sec.rate.name: Read-ahead pages per second
mssql.readahead_pages_sec.rate.description: Indicates the number of pages read per second in anticipation of use.

mssql.safe_autoparams_sec.rate.name: Safe auto-params per second
mssql.safe_autoparams_sec.rate.description: Number of safe auto-parameterization attempts per second. Safe refers to a determination that a cached execution plan can be shared between different similar-looking Transact-SQL statements. SQL Server makes many auto-parameterization attempts, some of which turn out to be safe and others fail. Note

mssql.sql_compilations_sec.rate.name: SQL compilations per second
mssql.sql_compilations_sec.rate.description: Number of SQL compilations per second. Indicates the number of times the compile code path is entered. Includes runs caused by statement-level recompilations in SQL Server. After SQL Server user activity is stable, this value reaches a steady state.

mssql.sql_recompilations_sec.rate.name: SQL re-compilations per second
mssql.sql_recompilations_sec.rate.description: Number of statement recompiles per second. Counts the number of times statement recompiles are triggered. Generally, you want the recompiles to be low.

mssql.table_lock_escalations.rate.name: Table lock escalations per second
mssql.table_lock_escalations.rate.description: Number of times locks on a table were escalated to the TABLE or HoBT granularity.

mssql.target_pages.name: Target pages
mssql.target_pages.description: The optimal number of pages in the buffer pool.

mssql.target_server_memory.name: Target server memory
mssql.target_server_memory.description: Indicates the ideal amount of memory the server can consume.

mssql.total_latch_wait_time.name: Total latch wait time
mssql.total_latch_wait_time.description: Total latch wait time (in milliseconds) for latch requests in the last second. This value should stay stable compared to the number of latch waits per second.

mssql.total_server_memory.name: Total server memory
mssql.total_server_memory.description: Specifies the amount of memory the server has committed using the memory manager.

mssql.transactions_sec.rate.name: Total transactions per second
mssql.transactions_sec.rate.description: Total number of transactions started for all databases per second.

mssql.unsafe_autoparams_sec.rate.name: Unsafe auto-params per second
mssql.unsafe_autoparams_sec.rate.description: Number of unsafe auto-parameterization attempts per second. For example, the query has some characteristics that prevent the cached plan from being shared. These are designated as unsafe. This does not count the number of forced parameterizations.

mssql.user_connections.name: Number of users connected
mssql.user_connections.description: Number of users connected to MSSQL Server.

mssql.workfiles_created_sec.rate.name: Work files created per second
mssql.workfiles_created_sec.rate.description: Number of work files created per second. For example, work files can be used to store temporary results for hash joins and hash aggregates.

mssql.worktables_created_sec.rate.name: Work tables created per second
mssql.worktables_created_sec.rate.description: Number of work tables created per second. For example, work tables can be used to store temporary results for query spool, LOB variables, XML variables, and cursors.

mssql.worktables_from_cache_ratio.name: Worktables from cache ratio
mssql.worktables_from_cache_ratio.description: Percentage of work tables created where the initial two pages of the work table were not allocated but were immediately available from the work table cache.

rabbitmq.healthcheck.alarms[{#SINGLETON}.name: Healthcheck: alarms in effect in the cluster{#SINGLETON}
rabbitmq.healthcheck.alarms[{#SINGLETON}.description: Responds a 200 OK if there are no alarms in effect in the cluster, otherwise responds with a 503 Service Unavailable.

rabbitmq.healthcheck[{#SINGLETON}].name: Healthcheck{#SINGLETON}
rabbitmq.healthcheck[{#SINGLETON}].description: It checks whether the RabbitMQ application is running; and whether the channels and queues can be listed successfully; and that no alarms are in effect.

rabbitmq.healthcheck.certificate_expirat.name: Healthcheck: expiration date on the certificates{#SINGLETON}
rabbitmq.healthcheck.certificate_expirat.description:
It checks the expiration date on the certificates for every listener configured to use the Transport Layer Security (TLS).
It responds with a status code `200 OK` if all the certificates are valid (have not expired).
Otherwise, it responds with a status code `503 Service Unavailable`.

rabbitmq.healthcheck.local_alarms[{#SING.name: Healthcheck: local alarms in effect on this node{#SINGLETON}
rabbitmq.healthcheck.local_alarms[{#SING.description:
It responds with a status code `200 OK` if there are no alarms in effect in the cluster.
Otherwise, it responds with a status code `503 Service Unavailable`.

rabbitmq.healthcheck.mirror_sync[{#SINGL.name: Healthcheck: classic mirrored queues without synchronized mirrors online{#SINGLETON}
rabbitmq.healthcheck.mirror_sync[{#SINGL.description:
It checks if there are classic mirrored queues without synchronized mirrors online (queues that would potentially lose data if the target node is shut down).
It responds with a status code `200 OK` if there are no such classic mirrored queues.
Otherwise, it responds with a status code `503 Service

rabbitmq.healthcheck.quorum[{#SINGLETON}.name: Healthcheck: queues with minimum online quorum{#SINGLETON}
rabbitmq.healthcheck.quorum[{#SINGLETON}.description:
It checks if there are quorum queues with minimum online quorum (queues that would lose their quorum and availability if the target node is shut down).
It responds with a status code `200 OK` if there are no such quorum queues.
Otherwise, it responds with a status code `503 Service Unavailable`.

rabbitmq.healthcheck.virtual_hosts[{#SIN.name: Healthcheck: virtual hosts on this node{#SINGLETON}
rabbitmq.healthcheck.virtual_hosts[{#SIN.description:
It responds with It responds with a status code `200 OK` if all virtual hosts are running on the target node.
Otherwise it responds with a status code `503 Service Unavailable`.

mysql.seconds_behind_master[\"{#MASTER_H.name: Replication Seconds Behind Master {#MASTER_HOST}
mysql.seconds_behind_master[\"{#MASTER_H.description:
The number of seconds that the slave SQL thread is behind processing the master binary log.
A high number (or an increasing one) can indicate that the slave is unable to handle events
from the master in a timely fashion.

mysql.slave_io_running[\"{#MASTER_HOST}\.name: Replication Slave IO Running {#MASTER_HOST}
mysql.slave_io_running[\"{#MASTER_HOST}\.description:
Whether the I/O thread for reading the master's binary log is running.
Normally, you want this to be `Yes` unless you have not yet started replication or have
explicitly stopped it with `STOP SLAVE`.

mysql.slave_sql_running[\"{#MASTER_HOST}.name: Replication Slave SQL Running {#MASTER_HOST}
mysql.slave_sql_running[\"{#MASTER_HOST}.description:
Whether the SQL thread for executing events in the relay log is running.
As with the I/O thread, this should normally be `Yes`.

mysql.seconds_behind_master[\"{#REPLICA..name: Replication Seconds Behind {#SOURCE.NAME}
mysql.seconds_behind_master[\"{#REPLICA..description: The number of seconds the {#REPLICA.KEY} SQL thread has been behind processing the {#SOURCE.KEY} binary log. A high number (or an increasing one) can indicate that the {#REPLICA.KEY} is unable to handle events from the {#SOURCE.KEY} in a timely fashion.

mysql.slave_io_running[\"{#REPLICA.KEY}\.name: Replication {#REPLICA.NAME} IO Running
mysql.slave_io_running[\"{#REPLICA.KEY}\.description: Whether the I/O thread for reading the {#SOURCE.KEY}'s binary log is running. Normally, you want this to be `Yes` unless you have not yet started a replication or have explicitly stopped it with `stop {#REPLICA.KEY}`.

mysql.slave_sql_running[\"{#REPLICA.KEY}.name: Replication {#REPLICA.NAME} SQL Running
mysql.slave_sql_running[\"{#REPLICA.KEY}.description:
Whether the SQL thread for executing events in the relay log is running.
As with the I/O thread, this should normally be `Yes`.
